claim: Left-handed people, on average, die younger than right-handed people.

result.claim: Left-handed people, on average, die younger than right-handed people.
result.reasoning: A follow-up study of Danish twins (Paper ID: 12) directly investigated the association between handedness and mortality. The study concluded, "We did not find evidence of excess mortality among non-right-handed adult twins in this follow-up study." Furthermore, the study reported that in 60% of dizygotic twin pairs and 50% of monozygotic twin pairs, the right-handed twin died first, which contradicts the claim that left-handed individuals die younger on average. While the paper lacks author, year, and citation information, its abstract directly addresses and refutes the claim based on its findings from a twin study.

evidence.text: The difference between the numbers of events occurring was statistically significant in every class of patients (p<0.001).
evidence.source: The preventive effect on respiratory tract infections of Oscillococcinum®. A cost-effectiveness analysis
